title: Dog Snapping at Air: Understanding Fly Biting Syndrome in Dogs
meta_desc: Learn about 'fly biting' in dogs, why it happens, and why this medium-urgency behavior requires a veterinary evaluation and video evidence.
content_html:
What is Fly Biting in Dogs?

Fly biting, also known as air snapping, is a repetitive behavior where a dog suddenly snaps their jaws at the air as if they are trying to catch an invisible insect. While it may occasionally look like play, it is typically an involuntary or compulsive action. This behavior can be caused by various underlying medical issues, including focal seizures (neurological), gastrointestinal distress, or behavioral disorders like canine compulsive disorder.

Is it an Emergency?

The urgency level for fly biting is classified as Medium. It is generally not an immediate life-threatening emergency unless your dog is also experiencing a full-body seizure, difficulty breathing, or extreme disorientation. However, it is not a behavior that should be ignored. You should schedule a veterinary appointment within the next 48 to 72 hours to begin diagnostic testing, as early intervention often leads to better management of the underlying cause.

Why a Video Helps Triage

Because fly biting episodes are often intermittent, your dog may not exhibit the behavior during their physical exam at the clinic. Recording a clear video of an episode is one of the most valuable tools you can provide. A video allows your veterinarian to analyze the dog's level of consciousness, eye movement, and muscle involvement. This visual evidence helps the vet determine if the issue is neurological (seizures) or related to another system, such as the digestive tract.

faqs.question: Can stomach problems cause fly biting?
faqs.answer: Yes, research has shown that many dogs exhibiting fly biting behavior actually have underlying gastrointestinal issues, such as acid reflux or irritable bowel syndrome.

faqs.question: Are certain breeds more likely to snap at the air?
faqs.answer: While any dog can exhibit this behavior, it is seen more frequently in certain breeds like Cavalier King Charles Spaniels and German Shepherds.

faqs.question: Is fly biting the same as a seizure?
faqs.answer: It can be. In many cases, fly biting is considered a 'focal seizure,' which is a seizure that only affects one part of the brain and results in localized muscle movements.
